[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}}, which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}}, which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}}, which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}}, which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}}, which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}}, which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}}, which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}}, which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}}, which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}}, which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the [[:en:Mikumo-Iwara_Site|Mikumo-Iwara Site]], which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * **Outside the jar** * 1 bronze sword * 1 bronze halberd * 1 small vermilion-stained jar * **Inside the jar** * 2 bronze spears * 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) * 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) * 3 glass magatama beads * Over 60 glass tubular beads * 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * **Bronze mirrors**: Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

revision.text:
[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the [[:en:Mikumo-Iwara_Site|Mikumo-Iwara Site]], which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * '''Outside the jar''' ** 1 bronze sword ** 1 bronze halberd ** 1 small vermilion-stained jar * '''Inside the jar''' ** 2 bronze spears ** 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) ** 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) ** 3 glass magatama beads ** Over 60 glass tubular beads ** 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings ** '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the [[:en:Mikumo-Iwara_Site|Mikumo-Iwara Site]], which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * '''Outside the jar''' ** 1 bronze sword ** 1 bronze halberd ** 1 small vermilion-stained jar * '''Inside the jar''' ** 2 bronze spears ** 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) ** 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) ** 3 glass magatama beads ** Over 60 glass tubular beads ** 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings ** '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * '''Outside the jar''' ** 1 bronze sword ** 1 bronze halberd ** 1 small vermilion-stained jar * '''Inside the jar''' ** 2 bronze spears ** 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) ** 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) ** 3 glass magatama beads ** Over 60 glass tubular beads ** 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings ** '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the [[:en:Mikumo-Iwara_Site|Mikumo-Iwara Site]], which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * '''Outside the jar''' ** 1 bronze sword ** 1 bronze halberd ** 1 small vermilion-stained jar * '''Inside the jar''' ** 2 bronze spears ** 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) ** 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) ** 3 glass magatama beads ** Over 60 glass tubular beads ** 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings ** '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the [[:en:Mikumo-Iwara_Site|Mikumo-Iwara Site]], which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/20171129102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-11-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * '''Outside the jar''' ** 1 bronze sword ** 1 bronze halberd ** 1 small vermilion-stained jar * '''Inside the jar''' ** 2 bronze spears ** 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) ** 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) ** 3 glass magatama beads ** Over 60 glass tubular beads ** 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings ** '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from 11.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013

[[File:Mikumo-Minamishouji Site, zenkei.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Mikumo Minamishouji Site ({{ill|Mikumo-Ihara Site|ja|三雲・井原遺跡}} Minamishouji District).]] {{Coord|33|32|12.1|N|130|14|29.6|E|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka Prefecture|relief=1| lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 32| lat_sec = 12.1| lon_deg = 130| lon_min = 14| lon_sec = 29.6| label = Mikumo Minamishouji Site| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption = {{Center|Location}}| float =right}} '''Mikumo Minamishouji Site''' (三雲南小路遺跡) is a {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} archaeological site located in Mikumo, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|ja|糸島市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the [[:en:Mikumo-Iwara_Site|Mikumo-Iwara Site]], which has been designated as a {{ill|National Historic Site of Japan|ja|国の史跡}}<ref>[http://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/050/030/bunkazai3.html "List of Historic Sites" (Itoshima City Website)]</ref>. The site is located behind {{ill|Saisa Shrine|ja|細石神社}} and includes a square burial mound (30 meters on each side) with two jar burials and a surrounding moat. It is considered a royal tomb of the ancient {{ill|Itokoku|lt=Ito Kingdom|ja|伊都国}}<ref name=Itoshima>{{Cite web|author=Department of Regional Promotion, Cultural Division|url=https://www.city.itoshima.lg.jp/s033/010/020/010/170/2017qq29102855.html|title=The Royal Capital of Ito Kingdom: "Mikumo-Ihara Site" Designated as a National Historic Site|work=Itoshima City|accessdate=2022-qq-19}}</ref>.
== Overview == In February {{ill|1822|ja|文政5年}}, Seishiro Mitoma accidentally discovered the site while excavating soil from a field at Minamishouji for building walls<ref>{{Cite book|author=Itoshima District Social Education Promotion Council|title=Ito Kingdom Archaeological Guidebook|publisher=Itoshima Newspaper|date=2001-4-30|page=52}}</ref>. In {{ill|1974|ja|昭和49年}}, a re-excavation revealed the "second jar burial." Academic investigations during the Heisei period confirmed the presence of a surrounding moat, identifying the site as a "square-moat burial mound" containing two jar burials. The jars are classified as "Tateiwa-style early-phase" from the middle Yayoi period. Ritual traces have been observed in the western moat, with potential links to the {{ill|Takazusayama mountain range|ja|高祖山}} to the east. These characteristics anticipate the features seen in the later {{ill|Hirabaru Site|ja|平原遺跡}} burial mound 1<ref>"Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref>.
Recent studies suggest that the Mikumo Minamishouji Site and the [[:en:Suguokamoto_Site|Suguokamoto Site]] burial with a large jar beneath a megalith in Kasuga City may have similar structural scales<ref>"History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013.</ref>.
== Grave Goods<ref>"Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi, "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985.</ref> == '''Jar Burial 1''' * '''Outside the jar''' ** 1 bronze sword ** 1 bronze halberd ** 1 small vermilion-stained jar * '''Inside the jar''' ** 2 bronze spears ** 31 or more bronze mirrors ({{ill|Han mirrors|ja|前漢鏡}}) ** 8 or more fragments of glass bi disks (lapis lazuli) ** 3 glass magatama beads ** Over 60 glass tubular beads ** 8 or more gold-copper four-leaf decorative fittings ** '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from 27.3 cm to 16.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned inscribed band mirrors|ja|異体字銘帯鏡}}, comprising 26 or more specimens.
'''Jar Burial 2''' * 22 or more bronze mirrors (Han mirrors) * 1 glass pendant (possibly reused fragments of a lapis lazuli bi disk) * 13 magatama beads (1 jadeite, 12 glass) * '''Bronze mirrors''': Sizes range from qq.4 cm to 6.0 cm. Most are {{ill|Inscribed mirror|lt=Linked arc-patterned "Sunlight" inscribed mirrors|ja|連弧文「日光」銘鏡}}, comprising 16 or more specimens.
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * "Rikuen Old Artifacts Analysis" by Munetada Aoyagi * "The Tragedy of the Golden Seal" by Dairoku Harada * "Mikumo Site: Minamishouji District," Fukuoka Prefectural Cultural Property Survey Report No. 69, Fukuoka Prefectural Board of Education, 1985 * "History of Fukuoka from Nature and Archaeology," Fukuoka City Historical Compilation Committee, 2013
